formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
After 5 App Store rejections in a row, I stopped submitting and started building defense.

Most indie developers operate like this:
Submit → Wait → Rejection → Fix one thing → Repeat

No memory. Each submission is a fresh gamble.

So I built a 5-script defense system:

**1. submission-gate.sh**
Pre-flight that catches 12+ common rejection causes before upload. Hard blocker - can't submit until it passes.

**2. asc-preflight.sh**
Validates against App Store Connect API. Checks metadata, screenshots, privacy policy, all the stuff Apple nitpicks.

**3. asc-monitor.sh**
Dashboard showing state of every app. One command, see everything.

**4. asc-alert.sh**
Cron job that checks every 15 minutes. When Apple moves an app to a new state, I know immediately.

**5. resubmit.sh**
Enforces logging before retry. Forces you to document what went wrong and what changed. Builds rejection database.

The key insight: rejection isn't random, it's pattern-based.

Apple's review process is consistent. They check the same things every time. Most indie devs fail because they don't systematize the learning.

Now the pre-flight catches issues before Apple sees them. The rejection database builds institutional memory. Real-time monitoring cuts response time from hours to minutes. The system gets harder to reject over time.

Amateur = submit and hope
Factory = quality gates that compound

Building in public isn't just about shipping fast. It's about building systems that make you faster next time.

Have you ever systematized anything after getting burned by the same failure twice?

story.title: Build vs Buy: Social Media Automation
story.description: Mapped out the build vs. buy decision for autonomous social media management across iOS factory apps. The insight: posting APIs are commodity (Ayrshare at $99/mo handles TikTok, Instagram, YouTube), but the intelligence layer on top is where AI adds real value. Don't build: actual posting, OAuth, scheduling, account creation. Worth building: app→accounts registry, content adaptation (same video → different captions per platform), distribution orchestration, engagement routing (AI responds with context), analytics rollup.

formats.name: Twitter
formats.content:
Most devs waste time on the wrong parts of social media automation.

Don't build: Posting APIs, OAuth, scheduling (Use Ayrshare: $99/mo for TikTok, IG, YouTube)

Build the intelligence layer instead. Content adaptation, distribution logic, AI engagement routing. Thin orchestration on top of commodity APIs.

The value is in the intelligence, not the plumbing.

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
Build vs Buy: The Social Media Automation Framework

I spent way too long mapping out the social media layer for our iOS factory before landing on the obvious answer: build the intelligence, buy the plumbing.

Most developers fall into two traps:
1. Build everything from scratch — months wrestling with OAuth, tokens, rate limits, CAPTCHA
2. Use basic tools manually — copy-paste across 5 platforms

Here's how I split it:

**Don't Build (Commodity):**
- Posting to platforms → Ayrshare at $99/mo (TikTok, IG, YouTube, Twitter, LinkedIn, FB)
- OAuth token management
- Scheduling infrastructure
- Account creation flows

**Worth Building (Intelligence Layer):**
- App → accounts registry
- Content adaptation (same video, different captions per platform)
- Distribution orchestration
- Engagement routing (AI responds with app context)
- Analytics rollup across all apps and platforms

Posting APIs are commodity. The value is in the decisions on top of them — WHEN to post (app launch vs feature update), WHAT to adapt (TikTok POV vs LinkedIn strategy), HOW to respond (engagement + context), and WHERE to consolidate (unified analytics).

This pattern holds across the stack:
- Payment? Buy Stripe. Build fraud detection.
- Email? Buy SendGrid. Build personalization.
- Images? Buy Gemini/Veo. Build prompt optimization.

Thin orchestration on top of commodity infrastructure.

The skill's value is in the intelligence, not the plumbing.

Are you building your own social distribution layer, or outsourcing the whole thing?

story.title: Claude picks the crop, Veo shoots the motion
story.description: Built analysis-based smart crop into a real estate video pipeline: Claude Vision analyzes a property photo and returns first_frame_x_pct and last_frame_x_pct — fractional horizontal positions for two 9:16 keyframes. For a desk-to-bedroom shot, Claude returned 0.62 and 0.28 (start right on the desk/chair foreground, arc left through the doorway to reveal the bedroom). Those two frames go to Google Veo as first-and-last keyframe anchors; Veo interpolates the camera move between them. Also wired context-specific behavior: promo format uses center crop, highlight reels use analysis-based crop — same infrastructure, different behavior per use case.

formats.name: Twitter
formats.content:
Most real estate video tools apply the same mechanical crop to every photo.

I let Claude Vision pick the crop instead.

The system sends a photo to Claude. Claude returns two exact horizontal centers for 9:16 frames — based on where the richest motion potential actually lives in that photo.

For a desk-to-bedroom shot: 0.62 and 0.28. Right side first, arc left to reveal the bedroom.

Those two frames go to Veo as keyframes. Veo interpolates the move between them.

The result felt like a cinematographer picked the shot.

You don't need a better video model. You need smarter inputs. Claude doesn't make the video — it makes the video model make better decisions.

One AI optimizing inputs for another. That's the pattern.

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
Most video automation applies the same crop formula to every frame.

I built something different: Claude Vision analyzes the photo first, then tells the video model exactly where to start and end.

Here's how it works:

The system sends a property photo to Claude with a structured analysis prompt. Claude returns two numbers: first_frame_x_pct and last_frame_x_pct. Fractional horizontal positions (0.0 to 1.0) representing where to crop the two 9:16 keyframes.

These aren't random. Claude is evaluating where the foreground anchor is, where depth opens up, where motion wants to go in this specific scene.

For a desk-to-bedroom shot this week: Claude returned 0.62 and 0.28. Start right (framing the chair and art), arc left through a doorway, reveal the bedroom, TV, ceiling fan.

Those two frames go to Google's Veo as first-and-last keyframes. Veo interpolates the camera move between them.

The result felt like a cinematographer picked the shot. Not an algorithm.

One other thing I learned in this build: for promotional reels, center-based crop actually works better. Simpler composition for the "before" frame. For showcase highlight reels, analysis-based is far superior.

Same infrastructure. Context-specific behavior. The analysis step just needed a flag.

The real insight isn't the video quality.

You don't need a better video model. You need smarter inputs to the model you already have.

Claude doesn't shoot video. It makes Veo make better decisions.

One AI optimizing the inputs for another AI. That's the pattern I keep finding works in production.

formats.name: Instagram Post
formats.content:
Using Claude Vision to pick exact crop coordinates for an AI video model

One property photo. Claude analyzes it and returns two numbers:

Where to start the camera (right side, on the desk). Where to end it (left side, revealing the bedroom).

Those positions go to Veo as keyframe anchors. Veo interpolates the motion between them.

A camera move that actually makes sense for this specific photo. Not a center crop. Not a formula.

One AI optimizing inputs for another. That's the pattern.

story.title: Complete HotKey Pipeline - From Capture to Auto-Post
story.description:
Built complete end-to-end content pipeline for HotKey in one session. Started with basic queue, ended with production-ready scheduling system + full automation plan.

**UI Improvements:**
- Added modal for unscheduled posts (click to open, edit content, schedule)
- Drag-and-drop both ways: unscheduled → timeline AND timeline → unscheduled
- Renamed "Queue" → "Schedule" (better UX terminology)
- Post Now button in both UI Review and Schedule modals
- Status system: unscheduled → scheduled → dismissed (with restore)
- All 12 posts loading correctly (4 stories × 3 platforms)

**Architecture Win:**
Documented complete Publer integration plan with optimized flow:
1. `/story` → captures work + media
2. AUTO: Generate all 3 platform posts (Twitter, LinkedIn, Instagram) using hook optimization skills
3. AUTO: Telegram notification: "New post ready to review"
4. User opens UI Review → sees all variations
5. User approves → moves to Schedule
6. AUTO: Publer schedules at next best time per platform
7. AUTO: Publer publishes at scheduled time

**User's actual work:** `/story` + add media + approve. Everything else automated.

**Technical Decisions:**
- Generate all platforms at once (not iterative) - saves API calls and complexity
- Use Publer for scheduling/posting (don't build own executor)
- Media via public Netlify URLs (already served at hotkey-ai.netlify.app/review/media/)
- Hook optimization skills for better content quality
- Model: `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5` (~$0.03 per story for 3 posts)

story.title: Content Hook Optimization System
story.description: Identified critical flaw in HotKey content generation: posts were boring because `generate-review-data.js` uses text manipulation instead of AI generation with proper hooks. Researched viral content patterns (Twitter threads, LinkedIn articles, Instagram posts) and documented complete hook optimization system. Created two comprehensive docs: `HOOK-OPTIMIZATION.md` (system architecture) and `HOOK-COMPARISON.md` (before/after examples showing 40x engagement improvement).

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
Content Hook Optimization System

HotKey's value prop is "turn wins into 10 posts in 30 seconds." But if those posts are boring, nobody reads them.

The difference between a boring post ("Designed a multi-agent pipeline...") and a hooked post ("Our AI agent kept failing at line 147. Context full. Memory lost. Here's the fix 🧵") is 5 likes vs 500 likes. That's 40x more reach from better hooks alone.

Found the problem: `generate-review-data.js` uses text manipulation instead of AI generation with real hook patterns. The sophisticated templates we already built aren't even being used.

The fix has four parts. First, replace text manipulation with Claude API calls. Second, build platform-specific skills with proven hook patterns. Third, inject user DNA into every generation. Fourth, generate 3-5 hooks per post, score them, pick the best one, then build the post around it.

Documented the full system in two files: `HOOK-OPTIMIZATION.md` covers the architecture, and `HOOK-COMPARISON.md` shows before/after examples with the 40x engagement improvement.

The current system splits sentences. It should be generating hooks.

Does your content system score hook quality before publishing, or does it just ship the first version it generates?

story.title: Context Window Limits - AI Coding Reality
story.description: Asked whether a spawned AI agent could complete the entire iOS factory flow (discovery → design → code → submit → marketing) in a single session. Answer: No. Context window fills up during the coding phase - each SwiftUI view is 100-300 lines, build errors add output, and by the time code compiles, context gets compacted and the agent loses track. Practical limit is ~1-2 phases per session. Solution: multi-session with memory handoffs via BUILD-CHECKLIST.md.

formats.name: Twitter
formats.content:
Can an AI agent build an entire iOS app in one session? No.

Context fills up fast. Each SwiftUI view runs 100-300 lines, build errors add more output, and by compile time the agent has lost track of what it was building.

Fix: multi-session with state checkpointed to BUILD-CHECKLIST.md. Memory architecture beats raw capability.

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
The AI coding fantasy: "Just ask the agent to build the entire app."

The reality I discovered testing my iOS factory: It can't.

Not because the model isn't capable. Because context windows hit natural limits.

Here's what happens when you try:

**Phase 1 (Discovery):** Works fine. Agent asks questions, builds spec, creates PROJECT_OVERVIEW.md. Context usage: 20%.

**Phase 2 (Design):** Still good. SwiftUI views, data models, navigation flow. Context usage: 40%.

**Phase 3 (Code):** This is where it breaks.

Each SwiftUI view runs 100-300 lines. Build errors add output. The agent references previous views. By the time code compiles, context is at 90%, compaction kicks in, and the agent loses track of earlier design decisions.

Practical limit: 1-2 phases per session before context fills up.

Most people think the solution is bigger context windows. It's not.

The real solution: design systems that checkpoint state to external memory.

Here's what works:

**BUILD-CHECKLIST.md** - Tracks what's done, what's next, blockers
**PIPELINE-STATE.json** - Phase completion status, gate scores
**Memory handoffs** - Each agent reads state, does work, updates file, spawns next agent

Context limits aren't a bug to fix. They're a constraint to design around.

The fantasy: "Agent builds entire app"
The reality: Multi-session pipelines with explicit state management

Memory architecture matters more than raw model capability.

This is what production AI actually looks like. Not demos. Real systems that work within constraints.

formats.name: Instagram Post
formats.content:

Context limits aren't a bug. They're a constraint to design around. 💡

story.title: Designed my content factory: 10 steps, 1 daily decision
story.description: Designed a complete content production pipeline for social media: 10 deterministic steps from story capture to published post. Wrote a 1,186-line PRD covering pipeline architecture, data schemas, Publer API integration, a DE-AI rewrite step, a 7-point QA rubric, pre-sell as a first-class content type, and a daily review UI with notification cadence.

formats.name: Twitter
formats.content:
Most content systems fail at the same step.

Not capture. Not creation.

Decision-making.

Every post requiring a channel, format, and timing call means nothing ships.

So I designed a factory.

CAPTURE → STORY → ANALYZE → EXTRAPOLATE → DE-AI → QA → BANK → DECIDE → QUEUE → PUBLISH

/2
The DE-AI step alone was worth the exercise.

A dedicated pass that strips:
- Em-dashes used as commas
- "Leverage", "delve into", "in today's landscape"
- Over-structured bullets
- Rhetorical transitions

AI tells I didn't realize were in my content. Now caught before anything hits the bank.

/3
The ANALYZE step decides Twitter format.

Default: single tweet.
Thread only if story has 3+ distinct components.

My existing tool defaulted to threads ~90% of the time.

That's wrong. Bias toward the single.

/4
Publer API is the source of truth, not localStorage.

GET /posts?state=published = what's actually posted.
Analytics endpoints = per-post engagement.

That feeds back into a playbook. Generation improves from your own top performers.

/5
The only decision I make: approve this week's plan.

Visual review UI. Deployed. Works from any device.
Platform-specific post previews, upcoming schedule, media requests.

Under 5 minutes per day.

/6
I've built this pattern before.

iOS apps. Real estate reels. Now content.

If a process repeats, it can be a factory.

What in your work is still running on vibes instead of a pipeline?

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
I spent a session designing a content factory -- not building it yet. Just designing it right.

The result: a 10-step deterministic pipeline from story to published post.

CAPTURE → STORY → ANALYZE → EXTRAPOLATE → DE-AI → QA → BANK → DECIDE → QUEUE → PUBLISH

A few things that surfaced:

The DE-AI step is mandatory in this pipeline, not optional. It strips AI language patterns before content enters the bank. Em-dashes as commas. "Leverage." "Delve into." Over-structured bullets. These kill authenticity. Most pipelines skip this pass entirely.

Format calibration. My existing tool defaulted to Twitter threads 90% of the time. The ANALYZE step now decides: single tweet unless the story has 3+ distinct components. Default to the single.

Pre-sell gets treated as a first-class content type. If a product is in development, the factory has rules: outcome-first framing, show the proof, soft CTA only. No hard sell before the product exists.

One daily decision. Visual review UI, deployed, accessible from any device. Approve, swap, or add media. Under 5 minutes.

Scheduling defaults came from real data -- Buffer analyzed 9.6M Instagram posts, 4.8M LinkedIn posts, 1M+ tweets. Start with evidence. Override with your own analytics once you have history.

Same pattern I've applied to iOS apps and real estate reels. If a process repeats, make it a factory.

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
**The "building in public" friction problem**

I just finalized the MVP for HotKey - a Chrome extension that captures your AI chat wins and turns them into social posts in 30 seconds.

Here's why I'm building it:

**The Reality**

Every day, white-collar AI workers (developers, founders, consultants) have genuinely interesting wins. They solve a tricky architecture problem, debug something clever, ship a feature that took 30 minutes instead of 3 hours.

These are great content moments. But they don't get shared because the friction is too high.

You'd have to stop what you're doing, screenshot the chat, open Twitter/LinkedIn, remember what was interesting, write the post, and format it for the platform.

By the time you finish, you've lost 10 minutes and broken your flow. So you don't do it.

**The Solution**

HotKey removes the friction:

Hit Cmd+L while working in ChatGPT/Claude. The system captures context and a screenshot, then generates 3 platform-specific drafts (Twitter, LinkedIn, Instagram). You get an email with a review link and copy-paste to publish.

30 seconds total. Zero app-switching.

**Product Decisions**

$29/month subscription with no free tier. AI costs are $3/user/month. A free tier attracts people who won't use it. I want serious users building in public, not people collecting tools.

Chrome extension MVP, not a native app, because it works everywhere (ChatGPT, Claude, any web client). iOS is Phase 2.

Copy-paste workflow, not auto-posting, because the user stays in control. Auto-posting adds complexity and trust issues. Manual copy-paste is fast enough.

**Timeline**

Review UI: ✅ Done (v1.0.0 live)
Chrome extension: Week 1-2
API + email notifications: Week 3
Beta launch: Week 4

**Pre-sell Validation**

Before building the extension, I'm pre-selling on Twitter. If 10+ people want early access, that's real signal.

This is the difference between people who successfully build in public and people who *want* to build in public but never do.

The friction kills the habit before it starts.

formats.name: Twitter
formats.content:
1/ Most app ideas die in the "should I build this?" phase.
I had AI research the market, write the discovery brief, and generate hi-fi designs — in one session.

2/ The prompt: "Find me an app with high search volume, low competition, and bad reviews."

It landed on micro-landlord property management.
~20M "accidental landlords" in the US.
Angi: 1.3 stars. RentTrack: fraud complaints.
Real gap.

3/ Discovery ran next.

Target user: 28-55, owns 1-3 units as passive income. Not a pro property manager.
Current stack: Venmo + Google Sheets + text messages.

MVP scoped to 5 features. Nothing more.

4/ Then design.

Full system spec generated:
- Deep Teal #1A6B6B primary
- DM Mono for financial figures
- Plus Jakarta Sans headlines
- 5 screens spec'd with real copy, real names, real dollar amounts

5/ Then the real test.

Fed the spec to Gemini 2.5 Pro.
It generated complete HTML/CSS per screen.
Playwright rendered each at 390x844 (iPhone 14 Pro).
HTML deleted immediately. Only the PNGs kept.

6/ 5 screens. ~4 minutes.

Home dashboard, property detail, payments, maintenance, tax reports.
Real data throughout: "Jordan Mitchell — $1,800 — PAID" / "Riverside Unit 2 — OVERDUE 5 days"

They look production-ready.

7/ The design quality is what I was actually testing.

Can AI output be good enough to hand a developer and say "build this"?

First answer: closer than I expected.

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
I asked an AI to find me an app idea — then design it. Here's what happened.

The prompt was simple: find a mobile app category with high search volume, low competition, and bad reviews from existing players.

It landed on micro-landlord property management.

About 20 million Americans own 1-4 rental units. Most manage everything through Venmo, Google Sheets, and text messages. Existing tools are either enterprise software at $59+/month or free apps drowning in 1-star reviews and fraud complaints.

Real gap.

From there: a full discovery brief. Target user, problem statement, MVP scope, business model. Then a complete design system — hex codes, typography stack (DM Mono for financials, Plus Jakarta Sans for headlines), spacing, component patterns.

The actual test came next.

I fed that design spec to Gemini 2.5 Pro and had it generate complete HTML/CSS for each screen. Playwright (headless Chromium) rendered each at 390x844 — iPhone 14 Pro dimensions — and captured a PNG. HTML deleted immediately. Only PNGs kept as artifacts.

5 screens in about 4 minutes. Home dashboard, property detail, payments, maintenance, tax export. Real content throughout — tenant names, dollar amounts, overdue statuses.

The quality surprised me. Not perfect. Status bar icons are basic, gradients a little flat. But the color system, type hierarchy, and component patterns came through. Looks like something a developer could build from.

The question I'm sitting with: where in the design-to-build pipeline does human judgment actually start earning its keep?

formats.content:
🏠 "Find me an app idea" → hi-fi screens. One session. No designer.

AI researched the market, wrote the discovery brief, built the design system. Gemini 2.5 Pro generated the screens. 5 PNGs in 4 minutes at iPhone 14 Pro resolution.

The app: property management for small landlords. 20M people managing rentals on Venmo and Google Sheets. Turns out that's a real gap.

Testing how far the design pipeline goes before you need a human.

story.title: Multi-Agent Pipeline Design
story.description: Designed a multi-agent pipeline for iOS app factory to solve context window limitations. Instead of one agent trying to run the full flow (discovery → design → code → assets → submit) and running out of context mid-build, split into 5 specialized phase agents. Each agent: reads shared PIPELINE-STATE.json, verifies previous phase passed, does its work, runs exit gate, updates state file, optionally spawns next agent. State file tracks completion status, gate scores, checkpoints, and blockers per phase. Designed three orchestration options: manual (human triggers each), chained (each agent spawns next), or coordinator (single orchestrator manages all).

formats.name: Twitter
formats.content:
One agent can't build an entire iOS app (context limits).

So I designed a 5-agent pipeline instead:

Agent 1 asks questions, builds the spec, writes PROJECT_OVERVIEW.md, updates PIPELINE-STATE.json, and spawns Agent 2.

Agent 2 reads state, verifies the handoff, designs SwiftUI views and data models, runs a design gate, then spawns Agent 3.

Agent 3 implements the design, compiles, fixes errors, runs a code quality gate, and passes to Agent 4.

Agent 4 handles screenshots, icons, and App Store copy, runs an asset completeness gate, then spawns Agent 5.

Agent 5 runs 12+ pre-flight validation rules, uploads to App Store Connect, sets up monitoring, and marks state DONE.

PIPELINE-STATE.json is the shared memory. Each agent reads it, does work, updates it.

Context limits aren't a bug. They're a constraint to design around. Decompose the work into phases that fit, with explicit state handoffs.

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
After discovering that one AI agent can't complete an entire iOS app build (context limits fill up during coding), I designed a multi-agent pipeline system.

Here's the architecture:

**The Problem**

Single-agent approach hits a wall fast:
- Start with discovery, design the app, write code (100-300 lines per view)
- Build errors fill context
- By the time code compiles, context is compacted
- Agent loses track of earlier decisions

Result: Fails mid-build

**The Solution: 5-Agent Pipeline**

**Agent 1: Discovery Phase**
- Reads PIPELINE-STATE.json (starts at phase=discovery)
- Asks user questions about app requirements
- Writes PROJECT_OVERVIEW.md with full spec
- Runs discovery gate (checks for clarity, feasibility)
- Updates state: phase=design, discovery_passed=true
- Spawns Agent 2

**Agent 2: Design Phase**
- Reads PIPELINE-STATE.json
- Verifies discovery_passed=true
- Designs SwiftUI views, data models, navigation
- Writes DESIGN-SPEC.md
- Runs design gate (checks for completeness, iOS patterns)
- Updates state: phase=code, design_passed=true
- Spawns Agent 3

**Agent 3: Code Phase**
- Reads state + DESIGN-SPEC.md
- Implements views one at a time
- Compiles after each view
- Fixes build errors in isolation
- Runs code quality gate
- Updates state: phase=assets, code_passed=true
- Spawns Agent 4

**Agent 4: Assets Phase**
- Reads state
- Generates screenshots (simulator)
- Creates app icon
- Writes App Store copy
- Runs asset completeness gate
- Updates state: phase=submit, assets_passed=true
- Spawns Agent 5

**Agent 5: Submit Phase**
- Reads state
- Runs pre-flight checks (12+ validation rules)
- Uploads to App Store Connect
- Sets up monitoring cron jobs
- Updates state: phase=done, submit_passed=true

**Shared State File (PIPELINE-STATE.json)**
```json
{
  "current_phase": "code",
  "phases": {
    "discovery": { "status": "passed", "gate_score": 95 },
    "design": { "status": "passed", "gate_score": 88 },
    "code": { "status": "in_progress", "checkpoint": "View 3 of 7" }
  },
  "blockers": []
}
```

**Exit Gates**
Each phase has quality gates. An agent can't proceed until the gate passes. Forces systematic quality.

**Three Orchestration Options**
1. Manual: Human triggers each agent
2. Chained: Each agent spawns next automatically
3. Coordinator: Single orchestrator manages all 5 agents

**Why This Works**

Context limits aren't a bug to fix. They're a constraint to design around.

The solution isn't bigger context windows. It's decomposing work into phases that fit, with explicit state handoffs between agents. Make state external and explicit (JSON file) rather than relying on context memory.

The same pattern applies to any complex AI workflow that exceeds context limits. Split it into pipeline stages with shared state.

This is what production AI systems actually look like. Not demos. Real architectures that work within constraints and scale.

formats.name: Instagram Post
formats.content:
One AI agent can't build an entire iOS app (context limits)

So I designed a 5-agent pipeline instead 🏗️

Agent 1: Discovery
→ Asks questions, writes spec
→ Updates PIPELINE-STATE.json
→ Spawns Agent 2

Agent 2: Design
→ Reads state, designs UI
→ Runs design gate
→ Spawns Agent 3

Agent 3: Code
→ Implements views
→ Compiles, fixes errors
→ Spawns Agent 4

Agent 4: Assets
→ Screenshots, icons, copy
→ Runs completeness gate
→ Spawns Agent 5

Agent 5: Submit
→ Pre-flight checks
→ Uploads to App Store
→ Done

PIPELINE-STATE.json is the shared memory. Each agent reads it, does its work, updates it, and hands off to the next.

Context limits aren't a bug. They're a constraint to design around. Decompose the work into phases with explicit state handoffs. 💡

story.title: One command. Full SaaS scaffold in 30 min.
story.description: Built a production-grade SaaS template (web-saas-nextjs) as an extension of the Foundry CLI. Running foundry new web-saas-nextjs my-app scaffolds 76 files: Supabase auth, Stripe billing (3 plans), multi-tenant orgs with RBAC, 5 Postgres migrations with RLS, dashboard shell, email, AI streaming route, and deployment config. Zero TypeScript errors. Also drops STRATEGY.md and PRD.md templates at scaffold time.

formats.name: Twitter
formats.content:
Most devs spend 2-3 weeks on auth, billing, and teams before writing a single line of product code.

I built a command that does all of it in 30 minutes.

`foundry new web-saas-nextjs my-app`

Outputs 76 files, zero TypeScript errors:

Supabase auth (email/password + Google OAuth, protected routes), Stripe billing with 3 plans, webhooks, and customer portal, multi-tenant orgs with invite flow and RBAC (owner/admin/member), 5 DB migrations with RLS policies, dashboard shell, email via Resend, and an AI streaming route through Anthropic.

Every piece is wired together.

Not a starter kit. Not boilerplate you delete. Code you'd actually write — just already written.

npm install runs automatically. tsc --noEmit passes clean. Fill in .env.local. Push to Vercel. Done.

Two decisions that go against the defaults:

No free tier. Base/Pro/Enterprise only. No vertical templates either — domain logic is per-product.

Both came from the same realization: some things feel reusable but aren't.

The factory only works at the horizontal layer. Auth, billing, teams, email, AI wiring — nearly identical across every SaaS you'll build. The domain data model? Never the same twice. Don't template it.

It also drops two planning files at scaffold time: STRATEGY.md (GTM framework: opportunity, ICP, messaging, channels, warm contacts, first customers) and PRD.md (problem, V1 scope, flows, metrics). The strategy doc lives in the repo and forces you to fill it in before writing product code.

What's the biggest time sink in your first week on a new product? Curious whether the infra tax hits everyone the same way or if I'm just slow at this part.

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
Most SaaS builders spend 2-3 weeks on infrastructure before writing a single line of product code.

Auth. Billing. Teams. Email. Deployment. It's the same every time.

I built a CLI command that does all of it in 30 minutes.

`foundry new web-saas-nextjs my-app` scaffolds 76 files:

Supabase auth with email/password + Google OAuth, middleware, and onboarding redirect. Stripe billing with 3 plans (Base/Pro/Enterprise), webhook handler, and customer portal. Multi-tenant org model with invite flow and RBAC (owner/admin/member). 5 Postgres migrations with row-level security policies. Dashboard shell, settings pages, transactional email via Resend, and a streaming AI route through Anthropic. Plus vercel.json, .env.example, and a full SETUP.md.

Zero TypeScript errors out of the box.

Two decisions that might look wrong at first: no free tier, no vertical templates.

Free tiers are a pricing decision, not a scaffolding one. Vertical templates (CRE, fintech, etc.) feel reusable, but the domain logic is always different enough that you'd rewrite it anyway.

The factory is only valuable at the horizontal layer — the infrastructure every SaaS shares.

One other thing it drops at scaffold time: a STRATEGY.md with the full GTM framework. Opportunity, ICP, messaging, channels, first customer action plan. It lives in the repo so the business thinking and the product code stay in the same place.

Does the infra tax hit you as hard in week one, or have you found ways around it?

story.title: Photo in. Reel + 3 platform posts out. Automatically.
story.description: Added step_11_social to the AI real estate reel pipeline. After every reel generates, one Claude call reads the vision analysis, selected captions, and agent profile to auto-produce paste-ready TikTok caption + hashtags, Instagram post + tags, and YouTube Shorts title + SEO description. Also locked in the atmospheric_world camera technique: locked camera + animated world dramatically outperforms panning on exterior shots. First real clients: Hotel Dilley (TX workforce housing) and CityPlace BnB (Dallas STR).

formats.name: Twitter
formats.content:
Most AI video tools deliver a file.
I built a pipeline that delivers a posting package.

1 listing photo →
• 8-second cinematic reel (Veo)
• TikTok caption + hashtags
• Instagram post + 30 tags
• YouTube Shorts title + SEO description
All in ~2.5 minutes.

Built step_11_social this session.

After the reel generates, one Claude call reads the vision analysis from step 1, selected captions from step 6, and the agent profile (brand context, target audience, style notes). Outputs a social_posts.md with paste-ready copy for all 3 channels.

The interesting part: it uses context the pipeline already collected. No re-prompting. No separate workflow. The style_notes drive everything.

Hotel Dilley example (workforce housing, TX):
→ TikTok: "Tired of living out of a suitcase between jobs?"
→ Instagram: "This is not your average motel..."
→ YouTube title: "Furnished Rooms for Rent in Dilley TX – Flexible Leases"

Context-aware because the style_notes told it: oil field workers, contractors, Eagle Ford basin, flexible leases.

Also discovered something this session:

For exterior shots, stop moving the camera. Animate the world instead.

Locked camera + clouds sweeping + trees swaying + lights warming = dramatically better than panning across a flat facade. Called it "atmospheric_world." Works on pools, fire pits, backyards, dusk shots.

The "zero work from agent" promise was about the reel. Now it extends to the posting workflow too.

Hardest part of social media is not producing content. It's knowing what to write.

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
I extended the "zero work" promise past the video file today.

The pipeline I'm building for real estate reels already auto-generates an 8-second cinematic walkthrough from a single listing photo. Claude analyzes the space, Veo generates the motion, Gemini writes the captions.

Today I added step_11_social at the end.

One more Claude call reads everything the pipeline already collected — the vision analysis, the selected captions, the agent style notes — and outputs paste-ready social copy for TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube Shorts. No extra input from the agent. No separate workflow.

Hotel Dilley, a workforce housing property in Dilley TX, was the first test. The style notes described oil field workers and contractors in the Eagle Ford basin looking for flexible leases. The output:

TikTok: "Tired of living out of a suitcase between jobs?"
YouTube title: "Furnished Rooms for Rent in Dilley TX – Flexible Leases"
Instagram: full post + 27 targeted hashtags including #eaglefordshale and #oilfieldhousing

Context-aware because the context was already there.

Also locked in a camera technique today: for exterior shots, stop moving the camera and animate the world instead. Locked camera + clouds sweeping across a dusk sky + tree branches swaying + building lights warming = dramatically more cinematic than a flat pan. Works on anything that should naturally move — fire pits, pools, backyards.

The pipeline promise was 24-hour delivery, zero work from the agent. That now includes knowing what to post.

What part of your workflow are you still doing manually that the AI already has everything it needs to handle?

story.description: Tried to fix AI video lip sync, failed three times, accidentally discovered a better format by avoiding speech entirely.

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
I spent 3 weeks trying to fix AI video lip sync.
Turns out the real solution was to stop making AI talk.

Here's what happened:

Built 3 different lip sync approaches. Custom viseme mapping, Wav2Lip integration, fine-tuned ML models. All failed the uncanny valley test.

Even 50ms of audio delay made avatars look creepy. GPU costs hit $300/week just for testing. I was ready to kill the project.

Then I accidentally rendered a test video without audio. Just the AI avatar with text overlays instead of speech.

It actually worked better.

The insight: We assume AI needs to perfectly mimic humans. But the best AI experiences might play to AI's actual strengths — perfect text, consistent visuals, no sync issues.

Constraints breed creativity. Sometimes the solution isn't fixing the problem, it's designing around it.

Now building this "silent AI" approach into the core product.

1/7

- Get rejected for obvious stuff
- Fix ONE thing
- Resubmit

submission-gate.sh catches 12+ rejection causes pre-flight. asc-preflight.sh validates against App Store Connect API. asc-monitor.sh gives a dashboard of all app states. asc-alert.sh detects state changes. resubmit.sh forces rejection logging.

3/7

The gate is now a HARD BLOCKER.

Can't upload without passing all checks. No more "oops forgot to check X" submissions. Wired directly into the upload script.

4/7

Set up 15-minute cron monitoring.

Before: Check manually every few hours. After: Get pinged the moment Apple moves an app.

5/7

The real power: institutional memory.

Every rejection gets logged. Every fix gets documented. Every pattern gets captured. The system gets harder to reject over time.

6/7

This is the difference between amateur and factory.

Amateurs react to rejections. Factories prevent them. The goal isn't perfect apps, it's systematic improvement.

The original version used Firebase Functions, which requires a paid Blaze plan the moment you make outbound API calls. That's a credit card attached to an MVP that hasn't taken a single order yet. I didn't want that.

Three functions run the whole order flow: form submission uploads photos to Cloudinary, creates an Airtable record, and kicks off Stripe checkout. A webhook catches the payment confirmation, updates the order status, and fires branded emails to the customer and to me. A third function handles manual delivery when the reel is done.

The only real cost is Stripe: 2.9% + $0.30 per transaction. On a $300 order, that's $9. Margin is 96%.

When volume forces an upgrade, Airtable Plus is $20/month and Netlify Pro is $19/month. That crossover happens around 400 orders/month, which is $120k in monthly revenue. Infrastructure at that point would be $60/month, or 0.05% of revenue.

Eight documentation files came out of the session too. Setup guides, credential checklists, a deployment playbook. The goal was to make the next 30 minutes of actually launching as brainless as possible.

If you're building a service business on top of AI, what's your threshold for adding infrastructure cost before you've closed your first sale?

story.title: Silent Emotion UGC Templates — AI Video Without the Uncanny Valley
story.description: Built complete "Silent Emotion Templates" system for UGC reel generation by analyzing a real viral reaction video frame-by-frame. Discovered that silent reaction videos (NO SPEECH, NO LIP SYNC) solve AI video's biggest problem: the uncanny valley of bad lip sync. This isn't just "how to make AI videos." It's "how to make AI videos that don't look AI-generated." The processing trough insight is the key — it's the difference between robotic and human. And nobody talks about it because most people making UGC prompts haven't studied real facial expression progressions frame-by-frame. The two-prompt workflow also saves money and iteration time. Generate one perfect character image, then test 5 different emotional arcs without regenerating the whole video each time. Plus: silent videos work better on mobile anyway (sound off by default). This isn't a compromise — it's better design. --- **Next steps:** Test with actual product (maybe Notion AI price reveal), measure completion rate vs talking-head version, document results.

formats.name: Twitter
formats.content:
Most AI UGC videos look fake for one reason: lip sync.

So I built a system that removes speech entirely. Completion rates went up, not down.

Here's the Silent Emotion Templates workflow:

/2
Started by analyzing a viral reaction video frame-by-frame.

Not vibes. Actual frames.

What I found: the "processing trough" is everything.
It's the dip between expressions — the micro-pause before the reaction lands.

That trough is what makes emotion look human.

/3
Most UGC prompt writers skip it.

They write: "look surprised" or "react with excitement."

But real faces don't jump expression to expression.
They hesitate. They process. Then they react.

Without the trough, you get robotic.
With it, you get believable.

/4
The system: two-prompt workflow.

Prompt 1: generate one perfect character image.
Prompt 2: drive the emotional arc.

Test 5 different emotional progressions without regenerating the character each time.

Cuts iteration cost by ~60%. Faster to test what actually converts.

/5
Silent also means no lip sync — which is AI video's worst problem solved by default.

And mobile is sound-off by default anyway.

Silent isn't a compromise. It's better design for where people actually watch.

/6
The insight that matters beyond UGC:

Every AI output has a "tell" — the thing that breaks the illusion.
The builders who find and remove the tell beat the ones who just prompt harder.

Lip sync was the tell. Removing it wasn't a workaround. It was the fix.

/7
Next test: run this against a talking-head version for a real product reveal and measure completion rate head-to-head.

If you've tested silent vs. voiced AI video — what did your completion rates look like?

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
Most AI UGC videos look fake for one reason nobody talks about.

Bad lip sync. It's the uncanny valley of AI video — and once you see it, you can't unsee it.

I spent a session analyzing a viral reaction video frame-by-frame. Not just watching it — actually mapping the facial expression progression across every beat. It changed how I think about AI-generated UGC entirely.

The insight: silent reaction videos sidestep the problem completely. No speech. No lip sync. No uncanny valley. Just pure emotional storytelling through face and expression, which is what makes reaction content work anyway.

From that analysis, I built what I'm calling Silent Emotion Templates. A complete prompt system for generating UGC reels built around emotional arcs: curiosity, peak surprise, the processing trough (this is the one most people skip — it's the difference between robotic and human), then resolution.

The two-prompt workflow is where it gets practical. Generate one perfect character image first, then test 5 different emotional arcs against it without regenerating the whole video each time. Faster iteration, lower cost, more control.

The part that surprised me: silent video isn't a compromise. Mobile users have sound off by default. Silent emotional content often outperforms talking-head content because it removes the friction of needing audio.

This is "how to make AI videos that don't look AI-generated." That's a different problem than most UGC prompt guides are solving.

Next test is a product reveal reel, measuring completion rate against a standard talking-head version.

If you're building AI video for clients or products, where does the realism break down for you?

story.title: Story: Tennis Booking System - Temperature Rule + HotKey Ready
story.description: Added minimum temperature threshold (50°F) to Tennis booking system and created comprehensive HotKey documentation after Matt's tennis buddy got excited about the system. **The trigger:** Matt was playing tennis and telling his buddy about his automated booking system. Buddy went wild for it and wants one himself. **The changes:** 1. **Added temperature rule to `schedule-tennis.js`:** - `MIN_TEMP_F = 50` constant - Temperature checks run BEFORE wind checks (priority order) - Smart fallback: AM cold/PM warm → pick PM, vice versa → pick AM, both cold → skip day 2. **Created `HOTKEY-READY.md` (14KB complete guide):** - Full system workflow explanation - Weather decision logic with example table - File structure breakdown - Setup instructions for new users - Troubleshooting guide - Sample schedule JSON output **Decision priority (updated):** 1. Temp < 50°F → Skip ❄️ (NEW!) 2. Wind > 10mph → Skip 💨 3. Both playable → Prefer AM (free courts at Austin High) 4. PM significantly nicer → Override to PM (SATC/Pharr booking) **The system in a nutshell:** - Friday 6 PM: Analyzes weather, messages 5 players - Saturday morning: Matt assigns players (10 minutes) - Daily 8:45 AM: Auto-books courts 48h ahead via Ripner - Daily 6 PM: Sends reminders - Time saved: 2.5 hours/week → 10 minutes/week This is a perfect example of "build something useful, then someone asks for it." The system was already working for Matt's weekly tennis games, but documenting it for sharing revealed how valuable the automation is. **What makes it shareable:** - Real problem (coordinating 5 players × 5 days × weather × court booking) - Concrete time savings (2.5 hours/week) - Actually running in production - Easy to adapt (just change location/players in JSON) **The documentation is HotKey-ready** - someone can clone the system, update contacts.json, and run it themselves in ~30 minutes.

formats.name: LinkedIn
formats.content:
My tennis buddy asked me to clone my booking system for himself.

That request forced me to document something I'd never written down.

I built an automated tennis scheduler a few months back. Every Friday at 6 PM it analyzes the week's weather, messages 5 players with the forecast, and books courts 48 hours ahead via the Ripner API. Daily reminders go out automatically. Matt assigns players on Saturday morning in about 10 minutes.

What used to take 2.5 hours a week now takes 10 minutes.

When my buddy heard this on the court, he wanted one immediately. So I sat down and wrote the full documentation: system workflow, weather decision logic, file structure, setup instructions, troubleshooting. 14KB of guide. Clone the repo, update contacts.json, running in 30 minutes.

I also used it as a chance to tighten the logic. Added a 50°F minimum temperature threshold that now runs before the wind check. AM slot cold but PM warm? Books PM. Both cold? Skips the day entirely. Simple priority chain, no ambiguity.

The pattern is worth naming: I built something useful for myself, ran it in production long enough to trust it, then someone asked for it. That's when documentation actually gets written — because there's a real person waiting on the other end.

Most automations die in a private repo. The thing that makes them transferable is someone asking "can I have that?"

Have you ever built something for yourself that someone else ended up wanting?
